chat.author: Kyle Rose
chat.text: I think there's more than a bit of cognitive dissonance in objecting to meetings in the US while not objecting to meetings in mainland China. Rule of law has been eroded to some extent in the US over the past decade, but (a) the courts have pushed back significantly, and (b) rule of law has never existed in Communist China. Perspective is helpful.

chat.author: Kyle Rose
chat.text: It seems like caprice is a bigger problem with a venue than published restrictions, except to the extent that the latter exceed some threshold for meeting viability. Your chances of becoming a political pawn and thrown into a prison for an indefinite period are much higher in China than in the US. It's not even close. And yet there's all this sturm und drang about the US. My response is: be serious, or at least be consistent.

chat.author: Grace Rachmany
chat.text: I can give first hand experience of how it is to enter Malaysia. You fill in an entry card in advance with all your passport details. At the border you scan your passport's first page like in every normal country and a camera does a facial recognition, and some glass doors open. There is no human looking at the stamps in your passport.

chat.author: Michael Richardson
chat.text:
Tim Chown said:

Side meetings are used for focused WG discussions, or have been

And, when that's really what's going on, it can almost always been done better as a group of six people at a pub.  Beyond 6, and adding remote people, and the discussion is useless, IMHO.

chat.author: Emil Lundberg
chat.text: Opinion on AI use in spec work (drafting/reviewing/etc): Please use it respectfully. I personally am here to read and hear the words of people, not bots. Using AI to _help_ you write (f.ex. if you're not as proficient with English as you'd like to be) is fine, but I would be very disappointed and a bit offended if someone told me they'd reviewed my draft and I then learned they'd had an AI summarize it for them.

chat.author: Tim Chown

chat.text: To just agree with Jim Galvin, I was able to get a side meeting slot for the OFDS side meeting we had today... I went in a few minutes after getting the message and was able to get the last slot available for Wednesday. And as I did that I watched other slots went away.

chat.author: Grace Rachmany
chat.text: At DIF our Steering committee put a general ban on AI contributions because of the IPR concerns. AI is notorious for IP violations, and there is no viable way for us as an open-source org under Linux Foundation to trust that the IPR is legit if an AI has made a contribution -- including ideas shared in mailing lists.

chat.author: Allison Mankin
chat.text: When I chaired and past-chaired nomcoms, I found that it was too easy for voting members to unconsciously consign some nominees to quick rejection, and it was easy for this to be the more diverse nominees. We interviewed everyone to help avoid this dynamic. And I understand that some subsequent nomcoms added diversity training, which is a good idea. I liked the point Jim made about encouraging nominees to accept their nominations even if they feel their case to be unlikely.
